authoritative_sources.title: National Custodial Specification
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA is an authoritative federal property operator. Its custodial specification defines custodial plans, service calls, frequencies, QA inspections, exposure-control requirements, complaint handling, and excluded-space boundaries for janitorial work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Work-order governance, execution logging, and QA inspection

authoritative_sources.title: Building operations and maintenance services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official GSA facilities-operations page requires a healthy cleaning plan, QA monitoring and auditing, exposure-control planning, EPA-recommended cleaners, and hazard-communication planning for custodial services.

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Workers Who Use Cleaning Chemicals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. workplace-safety regulator. This janitor-focused OSHA-NIOSH infosheet defines chemical hazard review, SDS access, worker training, dilution, ventilation, PPE, and label requirements that gate safe janitorial execution.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Chemical, PPE, and ventilation readiness review
used_for: cleaner versus sanitizer versus disinfectant terminology
SDS, labeling, dilution, and training prerequisites
refusal when unsafe mixing or ventilation conditions exist

authoritative_sources.title: Most frequently asked questions concerning the bloodborne pathogens standard
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official OSHA interpretation explains when janitors, housekeepers, and maintenance workers have occupational exposure and what exposure-control, housekeeping, regulated-waste, and decontamination measures must exist.

authoritative_sources.title: Identifying Greener Cleaning Products
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA is the primary U.S. environmental regulator for antimicrobial pesticide registration and greener-cleaning guidance. This source defines safer-product selection, chemical-management expectations, label clarity, and the boundary that disinfectants should be limited to appropriate contaminated high-touch uses.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Approved product selection and disinfectant-use controls

authoritative_sources.title: Mold Remediation in Schools and Commercial Buildings Guide: Chapter 1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA's mold guidance is a primary public baseline for schools and commercial buildings. It is written for building managers and custodians and helps determine when moisture or mold situations can be handled in-house versus requiring a separate remediation plan or outside contractor.
used_for: completion blockers tied to unresolved moisture conditions

authoritative_sources.title: Service Level Agreements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The University of Kansas is a public institution operating a custodial-services program. Its public SLA provides a vendor-neutral operating reference for quantified service levels, monthly QA walkthroughs, and work-request routing used to keep the lane specific to janitorial operations.

workflow_stages.name: Work intake, zoning, and readiness normalization
workflow_stages.description: Classify the janitorial request, normalize facility and zone context, confirm room or route scope, and verify that the work order and service-level baseline are specific enough to act on safely.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Chemical, supply, access, and occupancy readiness review
workflow_stages.description: Confirm approved products, SDS access, dilution methods, PPE, inventory, access windows, escort or room-release status, and occupant-impact controls before work advances.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Execution logging and service verification
workflow_stages.description: Track route execution, porter calls, restroom servicing, waste removal, and floor-care activity through logs, timestamps, counts, photos, and service notes tied back to the governing work order.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: QA inspection, deficiency, and rework handling
workflow_stages.description: Use QA walkthroughs, complaint tickets, deficiency logs, and rework packages to decide whether janitorial work can stay in progress, must rework, or should remain blocked.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Completion confirmation and specialist escalation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm that closure criteria, QA evidence, and safety posture support completion; escalate when the request crosses into mold remediation, bloodborne exposure handling, licensed trade, code, or approval authority.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

artifacts: work orders, route sheets, room or zone lists, and service-level agreements
approved product lists, SDS records, dilution procedures, exposure-control plans, and PPE requirements
inventory counts, replenishment logs, equipment status notes, and consumable exception records
access approvals, restricted-area releases, escort plans, and occupancy or event calendars
QA walkthroughs, inspection scorecards, deficiency logs, complaint tickets, and rework records
spill reports, blood or OPIM incident notes, mold or moisture observations, and safety alerts
completion photos, timestamped service logs, closeout checklists, and escalated handoff notes
systems_of_record: janitorial route scheduling or shift assignment systems
EHS, incident, and complaint management systems

allowed: Classify janitorial work as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Advance, hold, rework, or complete work based on service logs, QA outcomes, access posture, approved products, and named completion evidence.
Produce evidence-backed status updates, completion records, service summaries, and escalation notes without claiming external approval authority.
escalate: Required room-release, restricted-area, chemical, inventory, or QA records are missing or materially conflict.
A spill, blood or OPIM event, mold or moisture condition, occupant-safety concern, or failed QA inspection changes whether work may proceed or close.
The request requires licensed trade judgment, code interpretation, pesticide or antimicrobial authority, environmental remediation, or staffing, spend, or policy approval.
refuse: Mark work complete without traceable QA, deficiency, or completion evidence.
Recommend chemical use, disinfectant scope, mold handling, or waste handling that exceeds approved products, public guidance, or tenant authority.
Approve trade readiness, code compliance, remediation sufficiency, or commercial decisions outside the lane.

domain_failure_modes: executing janitorial work with unlabeled chemicals, missing SDS access, unsafe dilution, or inadequate ventilation controls
treating disinfectants as default cleaning products when the work only calls for routine cleaning
servicing restricted or still-occupied areas without confirmed release, escort, or occupant-impact controls
closing janitorial work after a failed QA walkthrough, unresolved complaint, or missing rework evidence
treating mold, moisture, blood, or regulated-waste situations as routine janitorial cleanup instead of escalating
allowing schedule or comfort pressure to override wet-floor, chemical, or exposure-control safeguards
collapsing janitorial operations into generic facilities support without documenting service-level, supply, inspection, and readiness dependencies
acting as trade authority, code interpreter, remediation authority, or final commercial approver instead of routing to the correct owner

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion
Tenant-specific adaptation preserving the public baseline

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Restricted-area access and after-hours authorization mechanics vary by tenant and sometimes by jurisdiction.
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: These rules materially change whether janitorial work is site-ready or blocked, especially in labs, child-care areas, food-prep spaces, or secure suites.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Runtime must retrieve tenant access and release workflows rather than infer them from the public baseline.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Bloodborne, medical-waste, and infection-control procedures vary materially by facility type.
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: The public baseline supports escalation and exposure-control posture, but tenant or sector-specific procedures determine whether janitorial staff may perform the cleanup directly.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack preserves a refusal and escalation boundary whenever those facility-specific controls are not provided.
